hindi: बड़ा मछली बॉल स्टिक पर (तला हुआ)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'fish ball' entry; no specific entry for deep-fried large fishball on stick. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM covers fish ball as a processed fish product; deep-fried variant is the closest defensible match. No exact code for fried fishball on stick exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for deep-fried fishball snack food.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one large deep-fried fishball on a stick, weighing approximately 80 g, as sold by Old Chang Kee in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo Database): Fish Ball, Fried
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo database entry for fried fish ball used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values are consistent with the input record (energy 250 kcal/100g, protein 10g, fat 18.75g, carbohydrate 10g). The input values were cross-validated against HPB Foodo fried fish ball data and retained where consistent. Sodium at 562.5 mg/100g is within the expected range for commercially produced fried fishballs. Trans fat (0.13g/100g) and cholesterol (25mg/100g) are carried from the input record; these are plausible for deep-fried processed fish products.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried fishball on stick. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the closest analogous food: processed fish cake / fish ball with starch coating, which contains tapioca starch and fish paste. Tapioca starch has a GI of approximately 54–70; the fish protein and fat content substantially blunt the glycaemic response. A conservative mid-range estimate of GI 52 is used, consistent with mixed fish-starch products in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 80g serving = (52 × 10g carbohydrate per 100g × 0.80) / 100 = 4.16, rounded to 4.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 562.5 mg per 100g — well above the 400 mg caution threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this snack; a single 80g serving delivers approximately 450 mg sodium, representing nearly 20% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended by HPB Singapore, and the deep-fried preparation adds excess saturated fat that further elevates cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High fat content (18.75g/100g) from deep-frying promotes insulin resistance; refined starch coating adds rapidly digestible carbohydrate with negligible fibre (0.63g/100g) to moderate glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit consumption of this deep-fried snack; the high fat load from frying promotes insulin resistance, the starch coating contributes refined carbohydrate with a low GI (~52), and the very low dietary fibre (0.63g/100g) provides minimal glycaemic buffering — a steamed fishball alternative would significantly reduce fat and improve glycaemic suitability.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (18.75g/100g) including saturated fat (3.75g/100g) and trans fat (0.13g/100g) from deep-frying, combined with high sodium (562.5 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this snack; the combination of high total fat, saturated fat, trans fat from deep-frying, and very high sodium (562.5 mg/100g) collectively elevates LDL cholesterol, promotes arterial inflammation, and raises blood pressure — all established cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly worsen joint inflammation; high sodium (562.5 mg/100g) exacerbates RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried fishball; AGE formation during deep-frying is a hard disqualifier for RA management, and the high sodium content further promotes tissue swelling — a steamed fishball preparation would be substantially more appropriate.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Big Fishball On A Stick (Deep-Fried) is a popular Singaporean street snack sold by Old Chang Kee, consisting of a large processed fishball made from fish paste (typically tenggiri or other white fish), tapioca starch, salt, and seasoning, deep-fried in vegetable oil and served on a bamboo skewer. The exterior is golden-brown and lightly crisp from frying, while the interior is dense, springy, and savoury — characteristic of the Singaporean fishball texture. Each piece weighs approximately 80g and delivers 200 kcal, 8g protein, 15g fat, and 8g carbohydrate per serving. The deep-frying process significantly elevates fat content to 18.75g per 100g compared to boiled or steamed fishball variants, and sodium is high at 562.5 mg per 100g due to the salt-heavy fish paste formulation. The snack is halal-certified and widely consumed as a hawker-style street food across Singapore. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension (high sodium), cardiovascular disease (high fat and sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGE formation from deep-frying). For Type 2 Diabetes, it is a moderate concern due to the high fat content promoting insulin resistance and the refined starch coating contributing low-GI carbohydrate with negligible fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low) based on analogous processed fish-starch products. A steamed fishball alternative is recommended for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: Is Old Chang Kee fishball healthy?
How many calories in a big fishball on stick?
Is fried fishball suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of fried fishball?
How much fat is in a deep-fried fishball?
What is the GI of fishball?
Is fishball on stick good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for fried fish ball (adapted match) and are consistent with the input record. The input was AI-generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood data; values have been cross-validated against HPB Foodo and retained where plausible. GI is an analogous estimate (no direct measurement for this specific product). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for deep-fried fishball is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific fried fishball product is not reported in the HPB Foodo database. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific deep-fried fishball product are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a deep-fried processed fish product with no vegetable ingredients; this is a naturally zero value consistent with the food type. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on processed fish-starch products in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific Old Chang Kee product. GL per 80g serving calculated as (52 × 10g carbohydrate per 100g × 0.80) / 100 = 4.16, rounded to 4.2. 6. Cultural metadata in the input incorrectly listed primary_cuisine as 'Curry Puff' — corrected to 'Singaporean' in this output. 7. Input had two portions both marked is_default: true; only one default portion (1 piece, 80g) is used in this output. 8. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed under HPB Singapore, which is the approved source; the URL is the HPB Foodo portal homepage. 9. Q8 fix (iteration 3): 'Da Yu Wan Chuan (You Zha)' added to flat aliases array to ensure every string in aliases_by_language.romanized appears verbatim in the flat aliases array. Self-check confirmed: all 19 entries in aliases_by_language (english: 6, chinese: 4, malay: 2, tamil: 2, hindi: 2, romanized: 3) are present verbatim in the flat aliases array.
